\title{Representation-Induced Spatial Structure in Culturally Clustered
Heritage Data:\\[4pt]
A Harmonic-Deviation Case Study with UNESCO World Heritage Sites}

\author{Seth Tenenbaum%
  \thanks{ORCID: \href{https://orcid.org/0009-0008-5797-2498}{0009-0008-5797-2498}.
  Correspondence: seth@fourthtemple.com. The author declares no competing interests.}\\[2pt]
  \small Independent Scholar, USA}

\date{}
\maketitle

% ==============================================================================
\begin{abstract}
% ==============================================================================

Coordinate-dependent scoring methods can produce stable within-frame
spatial structure in culturally clustered datasets.
This paper develops that structure as a methodological object using the
UNESCO Cultural and Mixed World Heritage corpus ($N = \NclusterTotal$ sites).
A harmonic-deviation scoring procedure selects a representation by sweeping
over reference longitudes and candidate spacings; the selected representation
lies near 3\textdegree{} spacing and the $\sim$35\textdegree{}E longitude
band. This selected spacing near 3\textdegree{} is treated as an exploratory
analytic spacing, not as a cultural, historical, metrological, or invariant
spatial period. Within that representation, diagnostics record reproducible
within-frame organization, including full-corpus cluster asymmetry,
phase-specific concentration, and stronger subset behavior among
domed/spherical monuments. The inferential hierarchy is explicit: these
statistics are post-selection diagnostics of the selected frame, not a global
discovery claim for invariant 3\textdegree{} periodicity. Invariant spectral
analysis does not detect such periodicity, so the result is interpreted as a
coordinate-frame effect produced by the interaction of clustered cultural
geography, harmonic-deviation scoring, and frame selection. Wikidata stupa
and OWTRAD route material are used as shared-representation consistency
checks, not as independent replication. The contribution is therefore
methodological: it shows how stable, reproducible selected-frame structure can
exist inside a coordinate representation without being an invariant spatial
period.

\bigskip\noindent\textbf{Keywords:}
representation dependence, spatial statistics, harmonic-deviation scoring,
UNESCO World Heritage, coordinate-dependent scoring, cultural analytics,
representation-induced spatial structure, monument distribution

\end{abstract}

% ==============================================================================
\section{Introduction}
\label{sec:introduction}
% ==============================================================================

Spatial analysis in archaeology and digital humanities rests on a set of
analytical choices that are rarely made explicit: the coordinate system, the
reference frame, the scoring or distance function, and the scale at which
proximity is evaluated. Each of these choices shapes what patterns are visible
in the data. When a corpus is geographically clustered, as most cultural
heritage datasets are, the interaction between clustering geometry and
analytical framing can generate stable, statistically detectable within-frame
patterns. Such patterns are representation-induced spatial structures: real
features of the data as seen through a chosen coordinate frame, with an
inferential status distinct from invariant spatial regularities.

Here, we use a case study to make the problem concrete. We apply a
harmonic-deviation scoring framework to the UNESCO Cultural and Mixed World
Heritage corpus. The frame is selected by a large parameter sweep over
candidate reference longitudes and spacings. Conditional on that selected
frame, the framework produces a reproducible within-frame concentration
pattern. The same pattern does not appear under an invariant spectral test.
This makes the case useful precisely because it separates two things often
conflated in spatial analysis: structure within a selected representation and
invariant periodicity of the point distribution.

The inferential distinction is central. The sweep over $(\omega,\phi)$ uses
the same corpus later evaluated by the diagnostics, so the reported
$p$-values are conditional on the selected frame. They quantify behavior
within that representation. They do not, by themselves, establish an
invariant spatial period. The spacing near 3\textdegree{} is likewise treated
as a selected analytic spacing, not as a demonstrated cultural, historical,
or metrological unit.

The methodological contribution has three parts. First, we provide a
reproducible case in which a coordinate-aligned statistic constructs a stable
within-frame pattern in a well-known public dataset. Second, we separate three
claim types: empirical observations about the corpus, conditional statistical
results within the selected frame, and methodological interpretations about
representation dependence. Third, we draw out implications for spatial
analysis practice in cultural analytics, where clustered heritage corpora are
common and representation choices are consequential.

\paragraph{Scope and framing.}
We treat the identified within-frame pattern as the primary object of study.
The pattern is \emph{empirically reproducible within the representation}, and
that reproducibility is meaningful at the selected-frame level. It is
\emph{representation-induced} in the sense that it requires the
harmonic-deviation scoring framework to appear and is absent under methods
that do not impose the same coordinate frame. Understanding why such patterns
arise is a practical concern for any researcher using coordinate-aligned
statistics on spatially structured datasets.

\paragraph{Paper structure.}
Section~\ref{sec:background} briefly reviews prior work on spatial periodicity
in monument distributions and introduces the harmonic scoring class.
Section~\ref{sec:methods} describes the data and methods.
Section~\ref{sec:results} presents the results in four subsections: conditional
within-frame diagnostics, shared-representation checks, representation
dependence, and sensitivity analyses. Section~\ref{sec:interpretation}
interprets why the pattern
arises and what it reveals about the framework. Section~\ref{sec:implications}
draws implications for cultural analytics practice.
Section~\ref{sec:conclusion} concludes.

% ==============================================================================
\section{Background}
\label{sec:background}
% ==============================================================================

\subsection{Spatial Periodicity in Monument Distributions}

The statistical evaluation of spatial periodicity in monument distributions has
a long and contested history \citep{thom1967,kendall1974,freeman1976}.
Probability-based frameworks for archaeoastronomical alignments were developed
by Magli \citep{magli2013,magli2016} and by Ruggles and Silva
\citep{ruggles1999astronomy,ruggles2015handbook,silva2020statistical};
the ICOMOS-IAU thematic study \citep{ruggles2010icomos} provides institutional
context for World Heritage analysis. A recurring methodological challenge in
this literature is distinguishing intrinsic spatial periodicities from
within-frame patterns induced by the scoring method, the corpus structure, or
the choice of reference frame. The present study addresses this challenge
directly by taking representation dependence itself as the object of study.

\subsection{Harmonic-Deviation Scoring}

A \emph{harmonic-deviation score} measures proximity by computing, for each
site, the angular distance to the nearest node of a candidate lattice. Given
a spacing $\omega$ and a reference longitude $\phi$, the harmonic nodes fall
at $\phi + k\omega$ for $k \in \mathbb{Z}$; the deviation for a site at
longitude $\lambda$ is $\delta = |\lambda - \phi| \bmod \omega$, folded
to $[0, \omega/2]$. Sites near nodes receive small $\delta$; sites near
anti-nodes receive large $\delta$. A scoring function then aggregates
individual deviations into a corpus-level proximity index.

This class of statistics is \emph{coordinate-dependent} by construction: the
score depends on the imposed lattice and on the selected frame. In a clustered
dataset, some values of $(\omega,\phi)$ will place lattice nodes near cluster
centres even if the clustering has no relation to the lattice period.
Optimizing over candidate frames can then select the frame that maximizes
this coincidence. Subsequent tests at that frame characterize the selected
representation unless the selection search is itself built into the null
calibration.

\subsection{Selected Reference Phase and Spacing}

The parameter sweep selects a frame near 3\textdegree{} spacing and the
$\sim$35\textdegree{}E longitude corridor. The 3\textdegree{} value is not
treated here as a culturally meaningful unit, a historical metrology, or an
independently specified period. It is the spacing retained by the scoring
procedure and is evaluated only conditional on that selection. The full
anchor-sweep correction is marginal ($p = \anchorMaxPermP$); the selected
frame is therefore used as an exploratory representation for conditional
diagnostics, not as a globally significant discovery from the full parameter
search.

The reference-longitude label is also descriptive. Both Gerizim
(\GerizimLon\textdegree{}E) and Jerusalem (\JerusalemLon\textdegree{}E),
landmarks \GerizimJerusalemKm{} km apart, fall in the same high-scoring
longitude band under the sweep, placing this corridor in the global top
\anchorSweepTopPct\% of \anchorSweepNanchors{} trial anchors. We label the
reference longitude by Mount Gerizim because it is not itself inscribed in the
UNESCO list (tentative ref.~5706) and therefore does not self-exclude from the
corpus. The label is geographic and procedural: the relevant empirical object
is the selected $\sim$35\textdegree{}E longitude band under the scoring rule,
not any individual landmark.

% ==============================================================================
\section{Data and Methods}
\label{sec:methods}
% ==============================================================================

\subsection{Corpus}

The UNESCO World Heritage XML dataset \citep{unescowhc2024xml} contains
$\NwhcTotal$ inscribed nominations. We retain Cultural and Mixed sites
(excluding $\NexcNatural$ Natural-only), drop $\NexcNoCoord$ entries lacking
geocoordinates (both linear route networks), yielding $N = \NclusterTotal$
sites. The corpus over-represents Europe and the Mediterranean-to-South-Asian
corridor (Europe and North America: \CorpusPctEurope\%; Asia-Pacific:
\CorpusPctAsiaPac\%; Latin America and Caribbean: \CorpusPctLatAm\%;
Arab States: \CorpusPctArabStates\%; Africa: \CorpusPctAfrica\%). This
geographic concentration is itself a structural feature that interacts with
the harmonic scoring, as shown in Section~\ref{sec:repn_dependence}.
Some UNESCO World Heritage properties are serial or multi-component
nominations, while the XML dataset often provides a single representative
coordinate. Longitude-based scoring therefore partly reflects the coordinate
structure of the corpus as published, not only the spatial extent of each
property.

\subsection{Harmonic-Deviation Scoring and Post-Selection Frame}

For anchor longitude $\phi =$ \GerizimLon\textdegree{}E and spacing
$\omega = 3$\textdegree{}, the deviation of site $i$ from the nearest
harmonic node is:
\[
  \delta_i = \bigl|(\lambda_i - \phi)\bmod \omega\bigr|,
  \quad \text{folded to } [0,\, \omega/2].
\]
The frame $(\omega, \phi) = (3\degr{}, \text{Gerizim})$ was selected by a
large parameter sweep: a scan over \anchorSweepNanchors{} trial anchors
(0\textdegree{} to 360\textdegree{}E, 0.01\textdegree{} resolution) and a
unit sweep over candidate spacings (0.5\textdegree{} to 15\textdegree{}).
The selected frame maximizes the harmonic-deviation enrichment score at a
selected analytic spacing near 3\textdegree{} and the 35\textdegree{}E
corridor. Full anchor-sweep permutation calibration yields $p = \anchorMaxPermP$
(\anchorMaxPermPSig{}) after full search correction for that sweep.
The selected frame is therefore used as an exploratory representation for
conditional diagnostics, not as a globally significant discovery from the full
parameter search.

All subsequent analyses are conditional on the selected frame. Their reported
$p$-values are post-selection diagnostics within the selected representation.
Holm adjustment below calibrates the within-frame diagnostic family; it is
not a substitute for a null model that replays the full frame-selection
procedure.

\medskip
\noindent\fbox{%
\begin{minipage}{0.94\textwidth}
\textbf{Inferential hierarchy.}
The analysis separates three levels of claim: empirical observations about the
UNESCO corpus; conditional diagnostics describing the selected
harmonic-deviation representation; and invariant-period claims, which would
require search-aware calibration or independent validation outside the selected
frame. The present paper argues at the first two levels and uses invariant
tests to delimit the third.
\end{minipage}}

\subsection{Diagnostic Tests}
\label{sec:tests}

Three threshold-free post-selection diagnostics characterize within-frame
concentration conditional on the selected frame (Holm step-down adjustment
within this diagnostic family, $k = \BonfK$). These tests ask how the selected
representation behaves.

\textbf{Test~1 (dome von~Mises mixture).} A two-component von~Mises/uniform
mixture is fit to the harmonic phase deviations of the domed-monument
sub-corpus ($N = \NcircTotal$; see Section~\ref{sec:dome_subset}), with the
mean fixed at the selected reference phase. Because the dome sub-corpus was
identified through an iterative keyword filter, this test maps how the
selected representation behaves in a culturally clustered subset; it is
exploratory subset characterization, not confirmatory evidence for a dome or
stupa placement claim. The mixture weight $\hat{w}$ quantifies the fraction of
dome sites concentrated near harmonic nodes within the selected
representation.

\textbf{Test~2 (Spearman cluster asymmetry).} Spearman rank correlation
between harmonic node mean deviation and site count, full corpus
($N = \NclusterTotal$), permutation null. Tests whether nodes drawing
more sites show smaller mean deviations, a signature of the cluster-node
interaction under the harmonic-deviation scoring framework.

\textbf{Test~3 (phase-rotation null).} The reference grid is shifted by a
uniform random offset in $[0\degr{}, 3\degr{})$; the test asks whether
the selected reference phase minimizes the full-corpus mean harmonic deviation
relative to random grid placements within the selected representation.

Supporting enrichment tests (Tests~4 and~5) use tier-threshold windows and
are not in the diagnostic family; they provide secondary within-frame
summaries.

\subsection{Tier Classification}
\label{sec:tiers}

Sites are assigned to descriptive tiers by their deviation $\delta$:
Tier-A++ ($\delta \leq \AppThreshDeg\degr{}$, $\lesssim\AppThreshKm$~km;
null rate $\NullRateApp$),
Tier-A+ ($\delta \leq \ApThreshDeg\degr{}$, $\lesssim\ApThreshKm$~km;
null rate $\NullRateAp$),
Tier-A ($\delta \leq \ATierThreshDeg\degr{}$, $\lesssim\ATierThreshKm$~km;
null rate $\NullRateA$).
Tiers are descriptive vocabulary within the harmonic-deviation representation;
no primary diagnostic depends on a tier cutoff.
Kilometer conversions are approximate aids: longitudinal ground distance
varies with latitude, and no latitude-adjusted distance metric is applied in
the harmonic-deviation score.

\subsection{Domed-Monument Sub-corpus}
\label{sec:dome_subset}

A keyword filter (\textit{stupa/stupas}, \textit{tholos},
\textit{dome/domed/domes}, \textit{spherical}) applied to site names and
UNESCO description texts yields $N = \NcircTotal$ sites. The keyword set was
refined iteratively during analysis; results from this sub-corpus are
hypothesis-generating and illustrative of the mechanism, not confirmatory
evidence independent of the keyword-definition process. Their role is
exploratory subset characterization within the selected representation. A
context-validated subset
($N = \NcircValidated$, \NcircContextRejected{} ambiguous sites rejected)
is used for sensitivity checks.

\subsection{Shared-Representation Check: Wikidata Q180987 Stupas}

A separately assembled Wikidata corpus of $N = \wikiStupaTotal$ stupa sites
(class Q180987) \citep{tenenbaum2026audit} is analyzed under the identical
anchor, spacing, and null model. This asks whether the same representation
organizes related data. Because the corpus overlaps geographically and
culturally with UNESCO, it is reported as shared-representation consistency.

\subsection{Simulation Nulls}

Three simulation approaches (\simNperms{} trials each, seed~$= \simSeed{}$):
(1) longitude permutation within the corpus,
(2) bootstrap from the full corpus,
(3) KDE-smoothed null modeling the observed longitude density.
A restricted geographic-concentration null (Null~C) additionally draws from
all full-corpus sites within $\pm W\degr{}$ of any dome site, providing a
footprint-matched reference distribution for the dome within-frame pattern.

\subsection{Reproducibility}

All statistics are generated by an automated pipeline; macros are regenerated
without manual editing. Scripts, data, and reproduction instructions are
deposited at Zenodo (DOI: \url{https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19938283},
v1.6.0) and catalogued in Supplementary~S1.

% ==============================================================================
\section{Results}
\label{sec:results}
% ==============================================================================

\subsection{Conditional Within-Frame Diagnostics}
\label{sec:results_emergence}

Results are organized according to the inferential hierarchy above. This
subsection reports empirical observations and post-selection diagnostics of
the selected $(\omega,\phi)$ frame. Section~\ref{sec:repn_dependence} then
asks whether the same pattern appears as invariant periodicity.

As an empirical observation, the full UNESCO corpus ($N = \NclusterTotal$)
has a Tier-A+ rate of \clusterApRate\%
($\NclusterAp/\NclusterTotal$; Clopper-Pearson 95\% CI
[\clusterApCIlo\%, \clusterApCIhi\%]), against the \NullRateAp{} geometric
null (Figure~\ref{fig:devhist}). The enrichment is
$\clusterEnrichAp\times$ the null rate, measured within the selected
harmonic-deviation representation.

\begin{figure*}[!t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth]{../figures/fig_devhist}
\caption{Harmonic deviation distribution for the full UNESCO corpus
  ($N = \NclusterTotal$) under the coordinate-dependent scoring framework.
  The shaded region marks the Tier-A+ window
  ($\delta \leq \ApThreshDeg\degr{}$); the dashed line marks the
  \NullRateAp{} geometric null. The excess of sites near nodes of the
  selected analytic 3\textdegree{} grid is the within-frame concentration
  pattern characterized here: a representation-induced structure visible under
  the imposed harmonic-deviation frame and evaluated against invariant comparison in
  Section~\ref{sec:repn_dependence}.}
\label{fig:devhist}
\end{figure*}

As a conditional statistical result, the selected analytic spacing near
3\textdegree{} is the highest-scoring parameter from the unit sweep. A
phase-envelope scan
(\phaseNphases{} phases at
\phaseStep\textdegree{} resolution) shows strong within-frame concentration
at the selected phase: full-corpus A+ counts span \phaseFullMin{} to
\phaseFullMax{} (median \phaseFullMedian{}). Among the \phaseNphases{}
evaluated phases at fixed selected 3\textdegree{} spacing, the selected phase
attained the maximum observed A+ count (\phaseFullGerCount{} A+). This
statistic is conditional on evaluating the selected 3\textdegree{} candidate
spacing: $p_{\mathrm{perm}} = \rayleighPermP$ (\rayleighPermPSig{}).

The three diagnostics record a consistent within-frame pattern in the selected
harmonic representation (Table~\ref{tab:primary_tests}).
\textbf{Test~1} (dome von~Mises mixture):
$\hat{w} = \targetedWdome$ ($\approx\targetedWdomePct\%$ of dome sites
concentrated at the selected phase under the harmonic-deviation framework;
$\hat{\kappa} = \targetedKdome$, angular spread
$\approx\targetedSdDomeDeg\degr{}$),
$p_{\mathrm{boot}} = \targetedPdome$ (\targetedPdomeSig{};
Holm $p_{\mathrm{adj}} = \pHolmTestOne$, \pHolmTestOneSig{}).
\textbf{Test~2} (Spearman cluster asymmetry):
$\rho = \spearmanDevClusterRho$, permutation
$p = \spearmanDevClusterP$ (\spearmanDevClusterPSig{};
Holm $p_{\mathrm{adj}} = \pHolmTestTwo$, \pHolmTestTwoSig{}).
Nodes drawing more sites show systematically smaller mean deviations,
the expected signature of cluster-node coincidence under the scoring
framework.
\textbf{Test~3} (phase-rotation null): the selected reference phase minimizes the
full-corpus mean harmonic deviation relative to random grid placements
($p_{\mathrm{perm}} = \fullMeanDevPermP$, \fullMeanDevPermPSig{};
Holm $p_{\mathrm{adj}} = \pHolmTestThree$, \pHolmTestThreeSig{}).
These diagnostics agree on the same selected-frame structure. The distinct
question of invariant 3\textdegree{} periodicity is addressed in
Section~\ref{sec:repn_dependence}.

\begin{table}[!t]
\centering
\caption{Selected-frame diagnostic and exploratory subset summary.
  Tests~2--3 are full-corpus post-selection diagnostics; Test~1 is retained as
  exploratory subset characterization because the dome/stupa keyword population
  was iteratively defined. Holm-adjusted values are reported descriptively
  within this selected-frame family.}
\label{tab:primary_tests}
\small
\begin{tabular}{lcrll}
\toprule
Test & $N$ & Raw $p$ & Holm $p_{\mathrm{adj}}$ & Direction \\
\midrule
Test~1 (dome VM mix.)
  & \NcircTotal & \targetedPdome\textsuperscript{b}
  ($w\!=\!\targetedWdome$, $\kappa\!=\!\targetedKdome$)
  & \pHolmTestOne~\pHolmTestOneSig{} & Positive \\
Test~2 (Spearman $\rho$)
  & \NclusterTotal & \spearmanDevClusterP\textsuperscript{b}
  ($\rho\!=\!\spearmanDevClusterRho$)
  & \pHolmTestTwo~\pHolmTestTwoSig{} & Positive \\
Test~3 (mean dev.)
  & \NclusterTotal & \fullMeanDevPermP\textsuperscript{b}
  & \pHolmTestThree~\pHolmTestThreeSig{} & Positive \\
\bottomrule
\multicolumn{5}{p{0.9\textwidth}}{\footnotesize
  \textsuperscript{b}permutation or bootstrap $p$.
  Holm (1979) step-down \citep{holm1979simple}.}
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

As an illustrative subset result, the iteratively defined domed-monument
sub-corpus shows a stronger within-frame concentration pattern than the full
corpus: \NcircTierAp/\NcircTotal{} sites at Tier-A+
(\NcircApRate\%, $\circEnrichAp\times$; Fisher $p = \pCircApFisher$
\pCircApFisherSig{}).
Tier-A++ concentration is more pronounced: \NcircTierApp/\NcircTotal{}
(\NcircAppRate\%, $\circEnrichApp\times$ the \NullRateApp{} null;
permutation $p = \circAppPermP$ \pCircAppPermSig{};
bootstrap $p = \circAppBootP$ \pCircAppBootSig{}). Because the keyword set
was refined during analysis, these results illustrate how the mechanism
appears in a culturally clustered subset; they are exploratory subset-level
expressions of the same coordinate-frame effect, not confirmatory evidence
independent of frame selection and keyword refinement.

\paragraph{Sites with high harmonic proximity.}
Tier-A+ domed monuments under the selected frame include Khoja Ahmed Yasawi
(0.2~km), Boyana Church (0.3~km), Lumbini (0.8~km), Humayun's Tomb (2.0~km),
Pyu Ancient Cities (2.3~km), Silk Roads: Chang'an--Tianshan (2.5~km),
Trulli of Alberobello (3.6~km), Old City of Jerusalem (4.1~km),
Borobudur (4.3~km), Kizhi Pogost (5.0~km), and Echmiatsin/Zvartnots (6.2~km).
These distances are harmonic deviations within the selected representation;
the kilometer labels are approximate because longitude degrees contract with
latitude.
They identify where the selected coordinate frame organizes the dome/stupa
subset most tightly across the Eurasian corridor.

\subsection{Shared-Representation Checks}
\label{sec:results_stability}

The following checks ask whether the selected representation behaves similarly
in related data. They assess shared-representation consistency. They are not
a separate discovery family, because the subsets and external corpus are
geographically and culturally overlapping.

\paragraph{Leave-$n$-out sensitivity.}
Within the exploratory dome subset, no single A+ site drives the conditional
result: removing any A+ site yields
$N = \LOOdomeN$, A+$= \LOOdomeAp$, $p = \LOOdomeP$.
Leave-3-out worst case across all $\binom{\LKOnAp}{3}$ triples:
$p = \LKOworstThreeP$. The A++ sub-population is less sensitive to removal:
leave-3-out worst case $p = \LKOAppworstThreeP$ (leave-2-out: $p = \LKOAppworstTwoP$).

\paragraph{Multi-scale enrichment.}
A Fisher combination across \multiscaleK{} log-spaced proximity windows
(null rates 0.33\% to 40\%) finds all windows enriched above their null
within the selected representation (OR $1.2\times$ to $6.7\times$;
permutation-calibrated
$p = \multiscaleDomeCombinedP$ \multiscaleDomeCombinedPSig{}).
Applied jointly across the UNESCO full corpus, UNESCO domes, Wikidata
stupas, and OWTRAD route nodes under the same harmonic frame, the maximum
joint enrichment peaks near \multiscaleJointMaxKm{}~km from a harmonic node
($p = \multiscaleJointMaxP$ \multiscaleJointMaxPSig{}). This is a descriptive
within-frame feature of the shared representation.

\paragraph{Wikidata Q180987 stupa check.}
Under the identical selected anchor, spacing, and null, $N = \wikiStupaTotal$
Wikidata-attested stupas yield \wikiStupaATierCount/\wikiStupaTotal{} Tier-A
sites (\wikiStupaATierRate\%, $\wikiStupaATierEnrich\times$ null;
binomial $p\ \wikiStupaATierBinomP$ \wikiStupaATierBinomStar;
permutation $p_{\mathrm{perm}} = \wikiStupaPermP$ \wikiStupaPermStar).
Harmonic nodes with at least one Tier-A stupa carry $\wikiStupaClusterRatio\times$
more stupa sites on average than empty nodes, in the same selected-frame
direction as the cluster-asymmetry diagnostic of Test~2. The Java/Sumatra node (105\textdegree{} to
115\textdegree{}E) concentrates \wikiJavaTightATierN/\wikiJavaTightN{} sites
at Tier-A (\wikiJavaTightATierRate\%; OR $= \wikiJavaTightATierOR\times$,
$p\ \wikiJavaTightATierP$ \wikiJavaTightATierPSig{}).

This is consistent with the same representation organizing geographically
overlapping Eurasian data, but it is not independent replication because the
corpora share geographic and cultural structure. The check remains within the
selected-representation hierarchy.

\begin{figure*}[!t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{../figures/fig_geo_trail}
\caption{Tier distribution of UNESCO domed/stupa and Wikidata Q180987 stupa
  sites along the Eurasian corridor under the harmonic-deviation scoring
  framework, overlaid on OWTRAD attested trade routes
  \citep{ciolek2004owtrad}. The figure shows where the selected
  representation organizes related Eurasian datasets, without assigning a
  harmonic encoding to routes or monument locations.}
\label{fig:geo_trail}
\end{figure*}

\subsection{Representation Dependence}
\label{sec:repn_dependence}

The preceding diagnostics show a within-frame pattern. The next checks ask
whether that pattern survives outside the representation that produced it.

\paragraph{Invariant comparison: Rayleigh test.}
As an empirical observation, the full-corpus Rayleigh test for
3\textdegree{} periodicity returns
$R = \fullRayleighR$ ($Z = \fullRayleighZ$,
$p_{\mathrm{perm}} = \fullRayleighPermP$ \fullRayleighPermPSig{}): no
spectral periodicity at 3\textdegree{} is detectable in the longitude
distribution. This comparison fixes the boundary of the claim: the reported
structure is a selected-frame concentration pattern, not invariant
3\textdegree{} periodicity. It appears when the harmonic-deviation scoring
function is applied with the selected phase.

\paragraph{Phase sensitivity.}
A $\pm 0.3\%$ shift from the selected 3\textdegree{} candidate spacing
collapses joint within-frame concentration across the dome sub-population.
This conditional result maps the specificity of the selected representation: the subset
pattern is sharply phase-dependent within the coordinate-dependent
framework. The phase-envelope scan shows that most grid placements do not
produce the observed count: among the \phaseNphases{} evaluated phases at
fixed selected 3\textdegree{} spacing, the selected phase attained the
maximum observed A+ count (\phaseFullGerCount{} A+), above the median
(\phaseFullMedian{} A+).

\paragraph{Clustered-control simulation.}
A clustered-control experiment assesses whether the unconstrained global
maximum over all $(\omega, \phi)$ is typical for corpora with realistic
dome-site clustering. The result is affirmative ($p = \clusterCtrlPermP$,
$N_{\mathrm{sim}} = \clusterCtrlNsim$): the free-sweep maximizer returns
$\omega = \clusterCtrlObsOmega\degr{}$, a spacing without metrological,
historical, or waypoint interpretation. This shows that free-sweep maxima and
selected-frame diagnostics are different methodological objects. The
3\degr{} spacing is therefore treated as the selected analytic spacing for
post-selection diagnostics, not as a unit recovered by unconstrained search.

Together, these findings support the methodological interpretation that the
within-frame pattern is a property of the selected representation. It arises
from the interaction of the scoring function with geographic clustering under
a specific coordinate frame.

\subsection{Sensitivity Checks}
\label{sec:results_robustness}

\paragraph{Geographic-concentration null.}
As an empirical observation, domed monuments are concentrated in the Eurasian
corridor
(\domeEurasianFraction\% in 20\textdegree{} to 120\textdegree{}E versus
\fullCorpusEurasianFraction\% of the full corpus). To assess whether this
footprint explains the within-frame pattern, we draw from all full-corpus sites
within $\pm W\degr{}$ of any dome longitude (Null~C) and compare dome A+
counts under the harmonic frame to this footprint-matched distribution:
$\pm 2\degr{}$ ($p = \geoNullDomeRestrictedPtwo$,
\geoNullDomeRestrictedPtwoSig{}); $\pm 5\degr{}$
($p = \geoNullDomeRestrictedP$, \geoNullDomeRestrictedPSig{}); $\pm 10\degr{}$
($p = \geoNullDomeRestrictedPten$, \geoNullDomeRestrictedPtenSig{}).
As a conditional statistical result, dome sites exceed the broader footprint
under this within-frame null. This strengthens the mechanism-level reading:
the selected frame organizes the dome subset more sharply than its longitude
footprint alone, while still remaining a post-selection subset analysis.

\begin{figure*}[!t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth]{../figures/fig_null_c}
\caption{Null~C bootstrap distributions for the context-validated dome
  sub-population ($N = \NcircValidated$, observed A+ $= \NcircValidatedAp$)
  at three window widths. For each window $\pm W\degr{}$, all full-corpus
  sites within $\pm W\degr{}$ of any dome longitude form the restricted
  pool; $10^5$ draws of size $N = \NcircValidated$ from that pool define the
  expected A+ distribution (blue bars). The observed count (red line) exceeds
  the pool mean at all three windows ($\pm 2\degr{}$:
  $p = \geoNullDomeRestrictedPtwo$; $\pm 5\degr{}$:
  $p = \geoNullDomeRestrictedP$; $\pm 10\degr{}$:
  $p = \geoNullDomeRestrictedPten$).}
\label{fig:null_c}
\end{figure*}

\paragraph{Anchor labels within the corridor.}
Both corridor landmarks produce similar within-frame scores: Gerizim
(\GerizimSweepAp{} A+, \anchorSweepPctile\textsuperscript{th} percentile)
and Jerusalem (\JerusalemSweepAp{} A+,
\JerusalemSweepPctile\textsuperscript{th} percentile) each place the
$\sim$35\textdegree{}E corridor in the global top \anchorSweepTopPct\% of
\anchorSweepNanchors{} trial anchors. Candidate anchors outside the corridor
(Mecca, Mt.~Meru, Mt.~Kailash) do not show comparable enrichment. This
supports the descriptive claim that the selected phase is a corridor property
within the representation. The landmarks function as labels for the selected
longitude band.

\paragraph{Americas negative control.}
The UNESCO Americas sub-corpus ($N = \AmericasN$) shows an A+ rate of
\AmericasApRate\%, \emph{below} the \NullRateAp{} null
($p = \AmericasOneSidedP$ \AmericasOneSidedStar{}, one-sided depletion).
The within-frame concentration pattern is absent under the harmonic-deviation
framework in a corpus without Eurasian cultural clustering, consistent with
the representation-dependence account.

% ==============================================================================
\section{Interpretation}
\label{sec:interpretation}
% ==============================================================================

The results identify a real selected-frame structure with three inferential
statuses. The empirical
observations are that the UNESCO Cultural and Mixed corpus is geographically
clustered, that the selected harmonic frame yields an excess of sites near
lattice nodes, and that an invariant Rayleigh test does not detect
3\textdegree{} periodicity. The conditional statistical results quantify the
within-frame pattern after the frame has already been selected. The
methodological interpretation is that the pattern is produced by the
representation rather than by an invariant spatial period.

\subsection{Why the Pattern Appears: Cluster-Frame Interaction}

The mechanism is the interaction of clustering, coordinate framing, and
scoring. The harmonic-deviation framework maps each longitude to distance from
the nearest node of an imposed lattice. Because the UNESCO corpus is clustered,
some lattice phases place nodes near dense regions of the corpus. The scoring
function rewards that proximity, and the parameter sweep selects the favorable
frame. This interaction is sufficient to produce a stable within-frame
pattern. The mechanism requires no assumption about intentional placement,
hidden geometry, or cultural metrology.

The analysis also keeps the 3\textdegree{} spacing at the selected-frame
level. We are not aware of a historical system that would independently
privilege a 3\textdegree{} longitudinal spacing, for example as a subdivision
of the \textit{beru} or related units. Such possibilities could be
investigated as separate historical questions; they are not needed to explain
the representation-induced structure reported here.

The same mechanism explains why the pattern changes status outside the
selected representation. The full-corpus Rayleigh test measures intrinsic
periodicity rather than proximity to an imposed lattice and detects no
3\textdegree{} periodicity ($p_{\mathrm{perm}} = \fullRayleighPermP$,
\fullRayleighPermPSig{}). That comparison identifies the observed pattern as
representation-dependent.

\subsection{Within-Frame Reproducibility}

Within-frame reproducibility is genuine and informative. Leave-$n$-out
checks, multi-scale enrichment, and related-corpus checks show that the
selected representation behaves consistently under resampling and in
geographically overlapping data. The scope of that reproducibility is the
selected representation; it is not, by itself, an invariant-period claim.

This distinction matters because resampling often functions as evidence of
stability in spatial archaeology. Here, resampling holds fixed the corpus
structure that helps create the pattern. The Americas negative control gives
the complementary observation: where the relevant Eurasian clustering is
absent, the same harmonic-deviation framework does not produce the
within-frame pattern. The appropriate conclusion is therefore methodological:
a coordinate-dependent statistic can yield a stable, statistically detectable
structure in clustered cultural data while failing an invariant test for
periodicity.

% ==============================================================================
\section{Implications for Cultural Analytics}
\label{sec:implications}
% ==============================================================================

The UNESCO case study identifies a general methodological lesson for
archaeology and digital humanities. Coordinate-dependent methods can be useful
for finding and describing selected-frame structure in culturally clustered
data. Their value is clearest when the analysis reports the frame selection,
the conditional diagnostics, and the invariant comparison as distinct parts of
the same inferential design.

\subsection{Where Care Is Needed}

Any spatial scoring method that measures distances or directional relations in
geographic coordinates implicitly assumes that the coordinate system is
meaningful for the cultural process under study. In clustered corpora, several
reporting points matter.

\begin{itemize}

\item \textbf{Post-selection overstatement.} If the same data select
$(\omega,\phi)$ and then test that selected frame, the resulting $p$-values
are conditional diagnostics. A search-aware null is needed for global
confirmatory inference.

\item \textbf{Suggestive spacing.} A selected spacing can acquire interpretive
weight simply because it is numerically memorable or historically resonant.
In this study, 3\textdegree{} is a selected analytic spacing. Cultural or
metrological interpretation would be a separate historical claim.

\item \textbf{Cross-corpus consistency without independence.} When multiple
corpora share geographic and cultural structure, as UNESCO, Wikidata heritage
inventories, and trade route databases do for the Eurasian corridor, showing
that the same within-frame pattern appears in all of them under the same
representation is shared-representation consistency, not independent
replication.

\item \textbf{Exploratory subset hardening.} Iteratively defined subsets, such
as the dome keyword subset here, can be useful for exploring how the mechanism
behaves in a cultural subpopulation. They should be reported as such unless
defined and validated independently.

\end{itemize}

\subsection{Methodological Recommendations}

Four practical responses follow.

\textbf{Report the search.} State how many frames, phases, spacings, anchors,
or thresholds were considered. Distinguish frame selection from subsequent
diagnostics.

\textbf{Use search-aware calibration.} When the goal is global discovery, the
null should include the parameter search. When the null does not include the
search, reported $p$-values should be described as conditional on the selected
frame.

\textbf{Include invariant comparisons.} A coordinate-aligned result should be
checked against at least one method that does not assume the same reference
frame. Disagreement, as in this case, locates the result as selected-frame
structure rather than invariant periodicity.

\textbf{Audit corpus structure and subset construction.} Geographic clustering,
corpus overlap, and iterative keyword selection should be treated as part of
the inferential design, not as background details.

% ==============================================================================
\section{Conclusion}
\label{sec:conclusion}
% ==============================================================================

This study identifies stable, reproducible selected-frame structure within a
coordinate representation. A harmonic-deviation scoring framework,
after a sweep over frame parameters, selects a representation in which the
UNESCO Cultural and Mixed World Heritage corpus shows a stable within-frame
concentration pattern. Conditional diagnostics quantify that pattern, and
related-corpus checks show shared-representation consistency.

The key comparison is representational. The pattern appears under the
selected coordinate-dependent scoring framework and does not appear under
invariant spectral analysis ($p_{\mathrm{perm}} = \fullRayleighPermP$,
\fullRayleighPermPSig{} for the full-corpus Rayleigh test). It is sensitive
to phase and spacing perturbations and absent in the Americas negative
control. The appropriate interpretation is therefore that the structure is
representation-induced: clustered corpus geography, coordinate framing, and
the scoring function jointly produce stable within-frame concentration.

The paper keeps the 3\textdegree{} spacing, the selected corridor, and the
dome/stupa materials in their proper inferential roles: selected analytic
spacing, coordinate-frame location, and shared-representation consistency. Its
contribution is to show how representation-induced spatial structure arises,
how post-selection diagnostics can be reported without collapsing into
invariant-period claims, and why invariant comparisons and corpus-realistic
nulls should be standard in spatial analysis of cultural heritage data.
The case is therefore best read not as evidence for a 3\textdegree{} heritage
periodicity, but as a reproducible demonstration of how coordinate-dependent
methods can create analyzable structure from clustered cultural geography.

% ==============================================================================
%  SUPPLEMENTARY NOTE
% ==============================================================================

\paragraph{Data and code availability.}
\label{sec:data_code}
All analysis scripts, data files, and reproduction instructions are archived
at Zenodo (DOI: \url{https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19938283}, v1.6.0).
The pipeline regenerates all statistics and LaTeX macros from raw data
without manual editing (\texttt{bash manuscript/reproduce\_all\_macros.sh}).
Full audit files, keyword lists, and supplementary figures are provided in
Supplementary~S1.

% ==============================================================================
%  APPENDIX
% ==============================================================================

\appendix

\section{Tier-Threshold Sensitivity}
\label{app:tier_sensitivity}

A log-scale null-rate sweep across \logSweepNtaus{} thresholds shows that
the exploratory dome pattern is not limited to the specific tier width within
the selected representation:
\logSweepNsig{} of \logSweepNtaus{} thresholds reach $p < 0.05$ for the
dome subset; dome OR ranges
\logSweepMinEnrichCanon{}$\times$--\logSweepMaxEnrichCanon{}$\times$ across
the three canonical tiers. Within $\pm$1 log-decade of the A+ threshold,
\logSweepAregionNsig{} of \logSweepAregionNtaus{} thresholds meet the
nominal $p < 0.05$ threshold.

\section{Von~Mises Mixture Characterization of External Corpora}
\label{app:vonmises}

A von~Mises + uniform mixture fitted to the harmonic phase deviations of
external corpora under the selected representation provides descriptive
within-frame summaries:
OWTRAD route nodes ($N = \vmNOwtrad{}$):
$\hat{\kappa} = \vmKappaOwtrad{}$, $\hat{w} = \vmMixtureWeightOwtrad{}$,
$\hat{\sigma} = \vmSigmaDegOwtrad{}^\circ$.
Wikidata Q180987 stupas ($N = \vmNStupa{}$):
$\hat{\kappa} = \vmKappaStupa{}$, $\hat{w} = \vmMixtureWeightStupa{}$,
$\hat{\sigma} = \vmSigmaDegStupa{}^\circ$.
$N$-weighted pooled $\hat{\sigma} = \vmSigmaDegPooled{}^\circ$;
$\kappa$ ratio $= \vmKappaRatio{}$ across corpora.
These values characterize external-corpus clustering within the harmonic
representation and are not used to derive tier boundaries.

\section{Keyword Audit}
\label{app:keywords}

\NcircKeywords{} morphological keywords from four roots (\textit{stupa/stupas},
\textit{tholos}, \textit{dome/domed/domes}, \textit{spherical}) matched as
whole words against site names, XML descriptions, and extended OUV text.
Context-validation for ambiguous terms: Gate~1 (\NdomeGateNeg{} negative
patterns) rejects non-architectural uses; Gate~2 (\NdomeGatePos{} positive
patterns) requires architectural confirmation.
Seven sites rejected in the context-validated subset:
Hiroshima Peace Memorial, Richtersveld, Gyeongju,
Vi\~{n}ales Valley, Rock Islands Southern Lagoon, Uluru--Kata Tju\d{t}a,
and Diqu\'{i}s Stone Spheres.
Full site-by-site audit in the Zenodo archive \citep{tenenbaum2026audit}.
